heading: XXVI SAVERIO CAMPANINI
card_summary: This chunk of Campanini's introduction to De Harmonia Mundi reconstructs Francesco Zorzi's pilgrimage to Palestine, which Campanini dates to 1493–94 based on internal evidence in the Commento sopra il Poema and a newly examined chronicle by the Milanese canon Pietro Casola. Campanini traces Zorzi's itinerary through Beirut, the Lebanese mountains, Acre (Ptolemais), Castrum peregrinorum, and finally Jerusalem, drawing on passages scattered across the Problemata and De Harmonia Mundi. Zorzi uses autoptic testimony—his own eyewitness experience—to corroborate scriptural geography, including Hebrew place-names (yam ha-maarav for the Mediterranean, yam ha-melah for the Dead Sea), the cedars of Lebanon used in Solomon's Temple, and the pomegranate orchards near Jerusalem evoking the Song of Songs. Campanini also notes Zorzi's alchemical interests and his melancholic reflection on the decline of Christianity in the Holy Land.
essay:
# Zorzi's Pilgrimage to Palestine: Autoptic Evidence and Scriptural Geography

## Overview

This section of Campanini's introduction to *De Harmonia Mundi* (pp. 26–31) is devoted entirely to reconstructing Francesco Zorzi's pilgrimage to the Holy Land. Campanini's method is philological and historical: he collects all passages in Zorzi's works — primarily the *Problemata* and the *De harmonia mundi* itself, but also the *Commento sopra il Poema* (the *Elegante Poema*) — that bear witness to the journey, and triangulates them against external documentary evidence. The section advances two interrelated arguments: (1) the pilgrimage can be dated with greater precision than previously thought, and (2) the experience profoundly shaped Zorzi's way of handling scriptural geography, providing him with personal, eyewitness (*autoptical*) authority to corroborate both biblical and kabbalistic topographical claims.

## Dating the Pilgrimage: The Casola Chronicle

Campanini reviews earlier scholarship on the pilgrimage's date. Ulderico Vicentini had inferred from the silence of Marin Sanudo's *Diari* (which document Zorzi's movements in detail from 1501 onward) that the journey must predate the sixteenth century. The *Commento sopra il Poema* — published after Vicentini — confirmed this, with Zorzi himself stating the pilgrimage occurred during his youth, specifically in his twenty-seventh year of life, i.e., 1493.

Campanini then introduces a previously unexamined source that complicates this dating: the pilgrimage chronicle of Pietro Casola, a Milanese canon who sailed from Venice to Palestine on 5 May 1494. On 10 September 1494, off Cyprus on the return voyage, Casola's galley encountered three Levantine trading galleys commanded by Giuliano Gradenigo. Among those who came aboard from the Venetian ships was a Franciscan friar from the Zorzi family (*"uno frate da le Zocole de Ca' Zorzi"*):

> Al tardo, se scoperseno tre galee del trafico de Levante, che venevano da Venezia, e gli era Patrono principale domino Iuliano de caxa Gradenico e se domandano le galee de Baruthi. [...] et inter alia, uno frate da le Zocole de Ca' Zorzi disse che a Venezia, la vigilia de santo Laurentio, publicamente, se diceva che a li XVI de agosto se aspectava a Milano el re de Franza e asai altre novelle.

<cite>Casola chronicle, quoted by Campanini, p. 27–28</cite>

This friar from the Zorzi household is travelling from Beirut toward Venice in September 1494. Campanini judges it improbable that a *different* Franciscan from the Zorzi family was making the same journey a year after Francesco — and therefore proposes a cautious revision: Zorzi may have misremembered not the year but his age at the time of departure. Rather than being in his twenty-seventh year (1493), he was in his twenty-eighth (1494). The correction is offered economically, without dismissing Zorzi's own testimony entirely.

## The Itinerary Reconstructed

Campanini reconstructs a probable route from passages in Zorzi's works:

### Beirut and the Lebanese Coast

Contrary to Maillard's hypothesis, Campanini argues that Beirut was the first port of call, not merely a departure point on the return. In *De harmonia mundi* Zorzi preserves a memory of the Franciscan church in Beirut, described as frequented not only by the friars but also by Maronites and adherents of the Roman rite:

> Fino ad oggi si sciolgono le lodi di Dio e del suo Cristo in Fenicia, in particolare nella città di Beirut, che si trova al centro di quella regione; là sorge una chiesa dei frati minori, frequentata non solo dai frati suddetti, ma anche dai maroniti e da quanti osservano il rito della chiesa di Roma.

<cite>De harmonia mundi, quoted by Campanini, p. 28</cite>

Zorzi also uses the Lebanon range as a topographical reference for Solomon's Temple, noting that Hiram of Tyre supplied its timber from the cedars of Lebanon — a mountain positioned, in Zorzi's precise geographical account, between Damascus and Beirut, extending from the Phoenician coast to the Maronite and Chaldean territories. The *Problemata* elaborate this further:

> Che cos'è il Libano? Non è forse questo monte della Siria che guarda Beirut alle proprie pendici settentrionali e Damasco dalla parte meridionale? Non lontano da quel massiccio scaturiscono due sorgenti, dette Ior e Dan, le cui acque si uniscono a formare il Giordano.

<cite>Problemata, quoted by Campanini, p. 29</cite>

This geographical precision, identifying the headwaters Ior and Dan as the sources of the Jordan, is characteristic of Zorzi's Origenian-style allegorical geography, in which literal topography is the substratum for spiritual meaning.

### Acre, Castrum Peregrinorum, and the Tribe of Zebulun

Moving south along the coast, Zorzi identifies Acre as ancient Ptolemais and Castrum Peregrinorum (modern Athlit) as a port within the territory assigned to the tribe of Zebulun at the foot of Mount Carmel. He also links Jonah's arrival from the fish to Ptolemais/Acre — a typological reading that fuses scriptural exegesis with personal geographical knowledge.

### Hebrew Place-Names and the Dimensions of the Holy Land

Campanini draws attention to Zorzi's use of Hebrew toponyms, noting his reference to the Mediterranean as *yam ha-maarav* ("mare occidentale") and the Dead Sea as *yam ha-melah* ("mare del sale"). A passage in the *Problemata* uses these designations to give the dimensions of the Holy Land: two hundred miles from Dan to Beersheba in length, eighty miles from the western sea to the salt sea in width.

### Jerusalem and the Song of Songs

The pilgrimage culminates with Zorzi's approach to Jerusalem, where he identifies the pomegranate orchards visible from the road — some ten miles from the city — as the likely model for the gardens described in the *Song of Songs*, again grounding scriptural imagery in autoptic experience.

## Zorzi's Melancholy Reflection on Christianity in the Holy Land

One of the most theologically charged passages Campanini excerpts concerns Zorzi's lamentation over the near-disappearance of Christianity from Palestine. The analogy drawn is striking: the Jewish people after the destruction of the Temple are compared to "water poured out," suddenly stripped of all divine gifts; the Church of Jerusalem has suffered an equally sudden decline, leaving behind only names and sacred memories. Zorzi cites Moses's blessing — *"Viva Ruben, e non muoia, benché siano pochi i suoi uomini"* — as prophetic of this vestigial Christian presence:

> Alcuni, ancora ai nostri giorni, si recano in visita a quei luoghi. Così vive ancora una fiammella di cristianesimo, perché si realizzi la profezia di Mosè: Viva Ruben, e non muoia, benché siano pochi i suoi uomini.

<cite>De harmonia mundi, quoted by Campanini, p. 30</cite>

## Alchemical Interests and the Mineral Poverty of Canaan

Campanini briefly notes that Zorzi does not entirely suppress his alchemical interests even in this geographical context. A passage in *De harmonia mundi* observes — again as autoptic verification — that the land of Canaan yields no iron ore, no copper, and no metal of any kind from its mountains, a fact Zorzi records as both empirically confirmed and spiritually significant.

## Significance for Zorzi's Project

Campanini's reconstruction establishes that the Palestinian pilgrimage was not merely a biographical episode but a methodological resource. Zorzi consistently invokes personal observation (*"abbiamo verificato di persona"*, *"abbiamo visto con i nostri occhi"*) alongside scriptural and patristic authority. This autoptic dimension distinguishes him from purely textual Kabbalists and aligns him with the Franciscan tradition of engaged, experiential piety. The journey also deepened his grasp of Hebrew geographical vocabulary and reinforced the Origenian framework — reading the literal landscape as the foundation for allegorical and anagogical levels of meaning — that structures *De Harmonia Mundi* as a whole.
